perturbation.original_text: Such invoice must be paid by the customer within 45 days of the date on which the invoice is issued.
perturbation.changed_text: Such invoice must be paid by the customer within 90 days of the date on which the invoice is issued.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment deadline to 90 days could create issues related to prompt payment laws, depending on the specific industry and jurisdiction (in this case Spain, where the service provider is located).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ley 3/2004, de 29 de diciembre, por la que se establecen medidas de lucha contra la morosidad en las operaciones comerciales
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 4.1 of the law establishes that the payment period agreed upon by the parties cannot exceed 60 calendar days after receipt of the invoice, unless otherwise agreed upon in writing. Changing the deadline to 90 days directly violates this law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a payment deadline of 45 days, while the changed text extends it to 90 days. The Law Explanation states that Article 4.1 limits payment periods to 60 calendar days unless otherwise agreed upon in writing, implying a violation when the deadline is unilaterally extended to 90 days. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is unrelated, the Law Explanation, in conjunction with the Perturbation Explanation highlighting potential prompt payment law issues, indicates that the changed text directly contradicts the legal constraint on payment deadlines.

perturbation.original_text: Except as provided hereinafter, for a period of five years after termination or expiration of this Agreement, each party shall not disclose, publish or disseminate information received from the other party which may be required to carry out this Agreement and which the disclosing party deems proprietary and confidential.
perturbation.changed_text: Except as provided hereinafter, for a period of one year after termination or expiration of this Agreement, each party shall not disclose, publish or disseminate information received from the other party which may be required to carry out this Agreement and which the disclosing party deems proprietary and confidential.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Spanish law. The adequate protection period for trade secrets depends on the specific facts but often needs to be longer than one year.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Ley 1/2019, de 20 de febrero, de Secretos Empresariales
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 5 indirectly supports the need for adequate confidentiality protection that might exceed one year, especially if the information qualifies as a trade secret whose economic value depends on its confidentiality. By setting a short one-year term, the contract might fail to provide the necessary means to prevent unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of trade secrets.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Article V | U.S. Constitution | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Article V Primary tabs The Congress, whenever two thirds of both houses shall deem it necessary, shall propose amendments to this Constitution, or, on the application of the legislatures of two thirds of the several states, shall call a convention for proposing amendments, which, in either case, shall be valid to all intents and purposes, as part of this Constitution, when ratified by the legislatures of three fourths of the several states, or by conventions in three fourths thereof, as the one or the other mode of ratification may be proposed by the Congress; provided that no amendment which may be made prior to the year one thousand eight hundred and eight shall in any manner affect the first and fourth clauses in the ninth section of the first article; and that no state, without its consent, shall be deprived of its equal suffrage in the Senate. ‹ Article IV up Article VI › U.S. Constitution Toolbox Explanation of the Constitution - from the Congressional Research Service
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year, which may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under Spanish law, according to the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation indicates that a one-year term might fail to provide the necessary means to prevent unlawful acquisition, use, or disclosure of trade secrets. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is not directly applicable, the core issue is the potential conflict between the contract's shortened confidentiality period and the legal requirements for trade secret protection, implying a legal contradiction as per the definition of Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Agreement rests, for all that, cancellable at any time by any of the parties before the expiry date of the Agreement or any of itsrenewals, upon three months prior written notice.
perturbation.changed_text: The Agreement rests, for all that, cancellable at any time by any of the parties before the expiry date of the Agreement or any of its renewals, upon one months prior written notice.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the notice period for cancellation to one month might violate general principles of good faith and fair dealing in contract law, especially if the other party is significantly reliant on the agreement. This is more relevant in Spain as contracts are normally longer than 1 year.
perturbation.law_explanation: Article 1258 of the Spanish Civil Code states that contracts are perfected by mere consent and that they obligate not only to what is expressly agreed upon but also to all consequences that, according to their nature, are in accordance with good faith, usage, and law. A one-month notice period could be deemed insufficient and contrary to the principle of good faith, particularly considering the long-term nature of the outsourcing agreement and the reliance one party may have on the continued provision of services. Setting a one-month cancellation notice, therefore, might be considered abusive.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-302. Unconscionable contract or Clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the notice period for cancellation from three months to one month, potentially violating the principle of good faith as described in the Law Explanation, which references Article 1258 of the Spanish Civil Code. The Perturbation Explanation also suggests that a one-month notice period might be insufficient and go against good faith, especially considering the long-term nature of the agreement. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses unconscionable contracts and clauses, it doesn't directly address notice periods, but it generally supports the idea that courts can intervene if a contract is deemed unfair. Based on the provided information and the Law Explanation, the change creates a contradiction because it may contravene the legally recognized principle of good faith.
